sentences.text: Well, once in Plaszow, we had to look-- we had to do something.
Otherwise, we would have gone to any menial work every morning, to knocking stones or whatever there is.
And it was dangerous not to do anything, because as the picture of Goth shows, if he saw somebody not doing anything, he was shooting from his balcony.
He was aiming.
So I worked in the-- in the-- in the bath house.
In the bath house, whatever they needed there.
They need to move something, they need to bring to-- to-- to repair some faucets, or whatever there is.
They needed somebody.
There was a big chimney near the bath house, and there was-- there was a stair-- a staircase, which [INAUDIBLE] you could go on the top.
[INAUDIBLE] I tried-- I tried not to be in the limelight so that nobody can see me.
Even one day, Himmler came in for a-- for looking over the camp, because this was, for him, a new camp.
And I-- at that time, I didn't want to be even on the main floor.
I went up on the chimney, and I stayed-- I stayed there.
Anyway, I started making-- I started looking for some job.
And since they told me about Emalia, I asked and I received an assignment to Emalia.
I also-- I also started later for my wife and for my sister, for all my family so that finally, when it came to the end, we all-- we were all at Emalia and we went together to Brunnlitz.
Including my brother-in-law, yes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My wife's mother died in the ghetto before-- before the-- before the liquidation of the ghetto.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- when I came to Emalia-- I want to give you a short history of Emalia.
Emalia belonged at that time to Mr.
Schindler, who was a [INAUDIBLE].
Mr.
Oskar Schindler received it as a-- for-- for his-- received it like any other German-- big Jewish factories.
However, Mr.
Schindler didn't throw out the owner, but he kept it, because Mr.
Schindler was not an industrialist like somebody-- like many people want to show him.
He was-- he was-- he was-- he couldn't be for himself.
He could do all the tricks that somebody wanted, but he didn't know how to care about a business.
And especially, he didn't know the business to begin with.
So he kept the owner of the factory.
The owner of the factory was Abraham Banker.
And he was the specialist.
He knew what it is.
I have to explain that in the beginning, the Emalia-- what it means, actually, which means that there's enamel work.
They made only enamel work.
And as long as they made enamel work, they got just a few workers, and they took some Jewish workers whom they paid whatever they're supposed to pay.
They came out from ghetto.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was hard work, because it was a heat-- tremendous heat all the time because, of course, enamel is being baked out and there's a heater on.
However, later on, Schindler-- so Schindler wanted to make some money.
With his contacts with the army, he-- he got-- he got steel.
He got steel-- steel-- steel supplies to make the pants-- button pants.
And, of course, whatever he needed they gave him.
However, he you couldn't have too much.
So he started-- his idea was to make also something which is needed for the army more, especially-- especially for-- for armament, some shells.
And he started building another holder with the machine-- with the stamp machine, and so on.
Since this-- and at the same time, he got-- he could request more of the-- of the metal.
Now, the metal-- from the metal which was remaining, he could made pots and pans for the black market, because this was not available.
It was a tremendous black market.
And this is where he started making money.
So he kept Mr.
Banker all the time.
Mr.
Banker survived all the whole war, and he went to Brunnlitz, of course, later on.
However, surprisingly to me, I did not find him or his name neither in the book nor in the film.
You're referring to--
In the film, the name of the person who is supposed to play his part is
Mr.
Stern.
And Mr.
Stern was a member of the judenrat, but he was in Plaszow.
He was not an enamel worker the whole time.
He came only on the list to Brunnlitz, where everybody wanted to get.
So he got on the list to Brunnlitz.
And I am surprised that he was substituted for the [?
Banker. ?] I don't know for what reason.
I never got any answer to it.
Anyway, Mr.
Schindler made a lot of money because the black market parts were a hot item.
And this was the money that he-- for which-- for which he made the parties for all his contacts in the army, in the SS, and even with Goth.
When he wanted something from Goth, he had to-- he had to give him, and so on.
So when we started going down to-- I mentioned when we started going out to Schindler-- to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to Schindler, we still went sometimes in groups because it was the beginning.
Right-- What was the name of the place where that Emalia factory was?
The Emalia factory was in Zablocie.
But in the beginning, we used to go down.
It was not such a big group of people.
But later on, he needed more people, and he stared building a camp, which means to build barracks and to build the camp.
This was near the factory, [? on ?] [? a ?] place.
And also, in the same camp-- in the same camp [INAUDIBLE] were the people who worked in the other factory in the NKF.
These people were also there at the same camp.
Schindler was a very good man from the beginning.
We didn't see anything wrong.
There were two engineers, which you have to be careful with them.
But he was a good man.
He was working every day in the factory.
He knew everybody by name.
He asked everybody how he feels.
He did not allow in the factory, or later in his camp, any-- he did not allow any German soldier to enter.
It was-- it was not too hard in the beginning because people didn't know about everything.
But later on, after the barracks were built already, it was very hard to get it.
But we came on time.
We took advantage of that Mr.
Banker knew some people with whom we were very close.
And through this person, through Mr.
Banker, whenever something was needed, we got it.

And we also had some help from the-- from the people who worked in the camp-- in the-- in the-- in the-- whenever a request came in, there was, like, an-- like, an-- you had to be-- somebody had to give you an advice to go there and to allow you to go there.
And these people-- what they call [GERMAN].
These people, we knew some people from there, and they helped us, also.
So together, as long as somebody wanted us, they put us-- they sent us there.
So I started working in a tool shop on a-- on a one of the [INAUDIBLE] machines.
And I learned this very quickly in one day, and [INAUDIBLE].
The good part of it was that we worked only during daytime, because otherwise, there were three shifts-- two shifts, actually.
Two shifts, not three.
And I worked always daytime, except at night when you needed-- when some emergency was and some tool broke down.
Then we had to go there during the night, and to repair it or to start making a new one.
My wife-- my brother-in-law worked also.
He worked also in the factory.
He was on the main room.
He was an engineer.
This was his part.
[INAUDIBLE] He was the first helper to the engineer.
Mr.
Schindler worked every day, and he knew everybody.
So far was he willing to help that if he knew that some people are smokers and they didn't have a cigarette, he started a cigarette and he threw it away.
Of course, he couldn't give it to anybody because he was afraid that he will be approached by the Gestapo [INAUDIBLE] for this type of behavior, which once happened to him later.
But he was freed.
After two days, he was freed right away.
I don't know the details, but I know that he was once arrested for his-- apparently, they-- they put something to his behavior that they wanted to arrest him.
But, of course, the people that he had, his people in the army, they freed him for the army supply.
My sister and my--
--my wife were working also.
My sister worked in the kitchen and my wife worked on the-- in the main room where they made shells, near the press.
The living condition was better because the food was better.
My sister worked in the kitchen for the director and for Schindler and for everybody.
There were a few people who worked there.
And she could always bring us something in the evening.
So it was already a big--
And my wife worked on this.
And while they were in the ghetto, they worked both at [INAUDIBLE].
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And in the-- in the ghetto, as long as they were in-- in Plaszow, they also worked for Madrich, which was before the [INAUDIBLE].
And Madrich-- and there were two people who were in charge of this factory there in ghetto-- in concentration camp Plaszow.
It was Madrich and Titsch.
And both of these people-- they wanted to help those who worked with them.
And at the end, they put them on the list to Schindler-- to go to Schindler.
Because they just liquidated this, and they went back to Germany.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So when these-- so Schindler-- Schindler was very good.
He showed us that he is not the typical German fellow like you-- like you-- like any others that we knew.
And at the very end when the-- from time to time, we had the radios and we knew the news.
We knew that something bad is happening to the Germans.
That they got a defeat in Stalingrad and that they are coming back.
So we knew also that the-- the Plaszow will be-- will be liquidated.
There were plans to liquidate Plaszow.
And because of this, Schindler had an idea that he would move the camp, and that we will go-- he will move all his workers there.
And we had to believe that he would be successful in doing it because it was hard to understand that he could move the factory there and move all the people.
He planned to move to Brunnlitz-- to Brunnlitz, which was in Sudetenland, and which belonged before the war to-- to Czechoslovakia.
But, of course, the Germans took it right in the beginning of the war.
They went in. ?] And there was a factory of felt in Brunnlitz, which makes hat.
Of course, nobody needed hats during the war, so they gave him this factory.
He knew about it.
And he moved out the machines-- very beautiful machines to make the top-- top felt for the hat.
And they brought our machine.
No, he wasn't moving.
He bought new machines there.
But here, when we stopped working-- before we moved to Brunnlitz, when we had to go to Brunnlitz-- everybody had to go up to the concentration camp Plaszow, because we couldn't sleep anymore.
There was [INAUDIBLE]
[INAUDIBLE].
We didn't work here anymore, so we had to go there.
And we stayed there about three or four weeks maybe.
Nothing.
Whatever-- whatever they gave us to do.
I mean, there is no choice.
Yeah, all together.
Some people were returned even before because they knew that they would not be on the list.
Schindler wanted to have families on the list.
And he tried this, and many families were saved this way.
So some people who worked in Zablocie did not get on the list?
Some people-- if they were single people or what, they didn't go.
They were not on the list.
You see, the whole-- all the other people who worked in Zablocie, the ones who were from NKF, they didn't get on the list.
However, a lot of people from the concentration Plaszow-- you know that there were-- that there were 300 women and 800 men.
So he didn't have so many men in this [INAUDIBLE].
Did people from Madrich factory get onto the Schindler's list?
Yes, they did get.
Yes.
As far as I understand, they paid for this.
Madrich paid for [? it-- ?] a lot of money.
And the money went to-- to--
--apparently to Goth, also.
It is good to mention it, that after we went-- this was-- this was already what I was talking about.
Now, this was in October or September, October '94.
Once we were already in Brunnlitz and we worked on the factory, we had a visit.
Mr.
Goth came in.

But nobody was already afraid of him because he didn't have-- he was arrested, probably for bribes or for whatever [? that is. ?] He didn't have his gun, and he didn't-- he wasn't-- he didn't have even his belt.
This was in 1944.
And this was the-- the-- the-- the month-- these were the months before we had to go to Brunnlitz.
But after they moved us to-- they moved us for a few weeks, for three or four weeks, to concentration camp Plaszow, and we had to wait there.
And, finally, the moment came in when we were supposed to go to Brunnlitz.
And we knew that we are going through Gross-Rosen, and the women are going through Auschwitz because that's-- that's the--
That's how they worked.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, when we went to-- when we were loaded on the cars and we went to Gross-Rosen, it didn't take too long because it was not a big trip.
And the women had to go later-- or later, or next day.
When we came to Gross-Rosen, it was the first time that we saw a real concentration camp. because--
--because Plaszow, we knew already.
But Plaszow were all people whom we knew, and everybody worked.
Everybody did something.
When we came to Gross-Rosen, it was a terrible situation.
When we saw the first day in the morning, the next morning-- when everybody had to go out and to be counted, when we saw the people who lived there, these were not people anymore.
No.
First of all, in the Emalia were not so many people.
They added a lot of people.
So they were added.
Apparently, they were-- I know some people who were there, and they don't know how they came there.
They didn't pay anything.
They didn't know that they are coming.
On the other hand, there were some people who-- from whom somebody wanted money.
And they wanted money, but they didn't have the money.
So either they came in or they didn't come in.
But the list was made, and there was-- it was different.
As far as we know, we don't know how Madrich people [INAUDIBLE].
Apparently, somebody paid for it.
I know about one family which paid.
They had money enough.
And they paid for this, and the whole family, four people, went on the list.
But it doesn't mean that this was the cause that the people who were in Emalia [? went on ?].
Because in Emalia, we didn't have so many people.
As a matter of fact, certain people from Emalia were liquidated-- were sent out upstairs to-- to the-- to the concentration camp before.
And as a matter of fact, once, an Australian plane fell on the Emalia.
On the Emalia barracks.
But, luckily, it hit the barrack which was emptied few days before.
And this was an [?
Australian ?] pilot.
And he was shot down, apparently, by--
He came during the night.
He was shot down by the Germans.
Well, Gross-Rosen was a camp that I would say there was-- they were-- these were not people anymore.
There was people so depressed that they couldn't look.
When they looked forward, you didn't know-- they were like mannequins.

They moved-- when they had to turn their-- their-- their heads to the left or to the right, you could see the whole-- all the-- whole-- the whole-- the whole camp was moving the same way, and nobody looked at you.
Nobody saw you.
Nobody looked.
When we came in, we came through-- we came through-- we came through a sauna.
We had to go for showers when we came in.
They told us to put everything on one place and to go to the showers.
Of course, everybody had something with him, and we knew that we wouldn't see this anymore.
But there was no time to do anything.
There were kapos and everybody, soldiers all around.
And we had to do it.
The first group went to the showers, and they had some towels.
The second group didn't have a towel.
When somebody asked for a towel, he got hit so that he didn't get out anymore.
And they kept us wet outside.
It was-- it was a cold November day already, or late October, whatever-- November.
And we were all-- we were all cold.
We huddled together.
Without any clothes.
Completely naked.
And, finally, [INAUDIBLE] when a group came together already, when there was enough people, they put us-- they took us to a barrack.
They were sitting-- first of all, they didn't give any clothes.
We were sitting there on the floor.
And then came another group.
And, finally, there was not enough.
Not enough room.
So they-- so they told us, we will show you that we will have room.
And they put people-- they told people to lie down, one onto another one.
And then he put one in between, somebody on the top.
And he jumped on him, and then he pushed him in.
And he showed us that they can do.
And, finally, they filled up the room and they started giving us some undershirt, whatever they gave us, and the uniforms.
And the uniforms-- of course, nobody asked already whether it's big enough or small enough.
Everybody took what he could, and then later, among themselves, they tried to-- and the next day, we came out on the-- they didn't take us to any work during these times.
We stayed about-- altogether, until we left, about six days, I think, or five or six days.
But we went every day out, and we saw the people who were working there.
As I mentioned, they were mannequins.
They were not people anymore.
In Gross-Rosen, about one week.
I don't know exactly the days.
And then they took us in cars and they brought us to Brunnlitz.
Cattle cars-- of course, cattle cars.
And there was nothing special-- there was not a far-- not far to go.
So we came into Brunnlitz.
It didn't take a few hours maybe.
And we [? debarked. ?] They took us out from the cars.
And we are waiting on the-- on the place-- in the place inside-- I mean, not in the rooms, because we didn't know whether we have rooms.
On the outside.
Schindler wasn't in somehow at that time, but there was-- [?
Lippold ?] was there, which was the commandant of the SS.
And he looked over everybody.
He counted us.
He had to have a list, and so on.
And the first thing what he did, he noticed a few children who were young children.
And he asked, whose are the children?
And so the parents came in.
And he showed us.
He put them on the side right away.
And the same day, he sent them to Auschwitz-- both the children and the parents.
Among other things was [INAUDIBLE] one names that can be known from Schindler's list, although they were never at Schindler's, was the--
The violinist Rosner.
And it was Horowitz, which was Rosner, also, I think-- these people who are here, who survived.
And they all survived.
They survived in Auschwitz because this was already the last-- last months of Auschwitz.
Auschwitz already discontinued--
Gassing people.
And so they came in there, and they survived.
And they came back.
No, I don't know exactly.
There are some people who know.
They were there.
He was-- he was one of the bad guys, anyway.
And we went there.
And we waited till the day was gone.
And then we went out to our-- to our rooms, which they had also double beds-- or I think even triple beds.
I think we had triple beds, because I remember-- and this was in the halls of the factory.
And I remember that once I was on the top, because it was, for me, easier to go on the top.
And I burned my back on the-- on the pipe, on the heat pipe, which was on the top.
No, the women were not there.
We waited for the women.
We didn't know why it took so long.
But, finally, they came in.
And when they came in, they went to their quarters, which was on the other side of it.
We had a partition of a door.
As a matter of fact, the top was open, so we could talk to each other, and so on.
And we started building the factory.
Yeah, they came in a condition-- they were shaved.
And they were-- of course.
They were shaved, and they came back to rooms.
Otherwise, they were just glad to see us, and we were glad to see them.
And we started working on the factory.
And the machines were put on already.
And some of us helped already to put them in.
And we started working, started production.
Schindler arrived in few days, in two days, I think, later, he arrived.
We arrived in Brunnlitz in November, beginning of November--
And we were there.
Yes, of course, there.
We had to-- we had to control everything.
Everything was controlled.
And I don't know the result of the control, but that's what [? this. ?]
We were a little bit relaxed, first of all.
First of all, we knew that-- [INAUDIBLE] especially when Schindler came back, we were relaxed.
We are going to work.
We didn't have far to walk.
We didn't have to-- nobody could come in to our inside, in the place, like it was on other things.
And Schindler was every day on the factory.
And he was very interested of everything which was done.
This was later on.
It was later on.
Later on, we got already-- we got one transport-- this was in the middle of winter-- from people they call them [GERMAN], which were from a camp which was liquidated.
And they were going from on camp to the other one if somebody would take them, but nobody-- nobody did.
They finally came to us.
And Schindler allowed to do it.
And they have to pry open the cars because they couldn't open them otherwise.
And they were-- half of them were frozen.
But the one who were alive, they took out, and they put them in the hospital.
We had two doctors there.
They put them in the hospital.
And they tried to revive them, and to bring them back to-- and they-- and they did come back.
In those cars, there were plenty of people.
